speaker: interviewer
text: You can start.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Our job was basically to cut down trees. These where huge trees and needed quite a know-how. And then once the trees were cut, we had to cut them up further and chop them off and haul them away and so forth. Also, we worked on the land. It depended, of course, what they needed. We had to do this all the time. We were quite far away. We lived kind of far away from the area where we had to work. And one particular morning as we were walking up the hill, a father of a friend of mine clutched his heart and collapsed. And he was dead of a massive heart attack. So he was our only casualty. And of course we had to bury him right by our house or our post. Now, our house consisted of two little rooms. One had a bunk-bed. As I mentioned, this was for the border guards. And the other one, we just slept there on the floor. And there was a small area where there was a stove. And one of the ladies was selected to do the kitchen work. And she prepared some and we took it with ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave you the food? Where did you get the food from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got a ration. We got a ration of food, which was like peas or beans and things of that nature. And I never forget, in the pocket of my jacket, I carried a little slip of garlic. And I was always wearing this thing and carrying with me, because in case if you get a little bread, then I would smear the garlic on the top of the bread. And then this would give me a great pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is how it was. And, as I mentioned, sometimes we got into the kitchen of the duchess and so forth. And we were able to get a little extra milk or extra little bread and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very careful to obey the rules. We were very careful about it. We worked very hard, very hard. But one particular occasion-- again, I think this was only the beginning of it, when I felt weak and I couldn't produce. This oberjaeger, the chief hunter, he wanted to slap me. And my mother jumped in front of me. And that was the end of that, because he respected my mother. He looked up to my mother because my mother, as I said, was educated in Germany. And she really spoke beautiful German. And these people appreciated that. And so that's why from that point on, I really didn't get into too much trouble with the German anymore, with the Nazi anymore. But we were watched. We were watched very closely. Now, the bad thing was the nights. First of all, we had an outhouse, which was a little bit further down. And the woods were full of animals. And we didn't trust the animals. We didn't know what kind of animals are there-- coyotes, wolves, bears. Everything was probably there. And we were afraid to go outside. But of course, sometimes nature calls and you have to go outside of the place where you are. And these were terrible times, because we were absolutely horrified from this. And this is how we were until about, oh, about March of 1945. And suddenly, we heard some shooting going on. And it turned out to be then we heard that the Russian start approaching, like 25, 30 miles away. And we were rounded up. And then the SS spoke to us. And he said, look at it. We'll be transporting you to another place further deep into Germany. But don't ever try to escape. And if you don't escape, if you don't try to escape, you will live through the war and you will survive the war. These were his words. And he was the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were taken to the station and we were transported. And we ended up in what is known as Theresienstadt. That was in Czechoslovak. How long were you in the forest all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest we were from, oh, July or August 1944 until about March of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about the Jewish holidays? I mean, were you able in any way to observe within your group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't believe we did, except, of course, as I mentioned, the leader was a very religious person. And he was praying in the morning and in the evening. But most of us were not. Most of us were not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for some reason or other, I don't think we really knew what day of the month we are at, where we are. But there's one little observation I have to tell you. One day when we woke up, my mother pulls me aside and she says, I had a terrible dream. I said, what do you mean? What was it? She says, I dreamt that my father-- your father is dead. He was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was just about the time, in October, 1944. And I told her, I said, don't worry about it. This is just a dream. Dreams don't mean anything. And it turned out to be that it was true. Somehow she sensed it. And from that point on, she acted like more introverted or somehow-- she seemed like a different person from that point on. Still the same caring person, but still it was-- you could tell by looking at her that she carries something with her, with inside--

speaker: interviewee
text: She felt a loss

speaker: interviewee
text: --which she cannot describe. So this is how it happened. And, as I said, in about March, 1945, we were put to the wagons again. And we ended up in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had no idea where you were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea whatsoever. It could've been Auschwitz or anything. We did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of Auschwitz yet? I mean, I've asked you that a few times, because I'm curious what you knew from the outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we heard in Strasshof we heard about Auschwitz. But we tried to get it out of our minds. We are not involved. We didn't hear anything. We didn't know where the Russian troops were, where the American troops were. We had no idea is Hitler winning the war or losing the war. We just simply didn't know. We had no connection with the outside world, nothing. And the only time when we heard was in Theresienstadt, really, when people I think smuggled in some kind of radio, a transistor radio or something, and they heard news. Now, in Theresienstadt, which as you probably know, which was a funny situation. They wanted to show the International Red Cross how nice the people live in there. That was a lot of bologna. When we arrived there, we understood that there is another extermination camp going to be built here and crematoria and what have you, and simply the Germans, the Nazis did not have enough time to execute their plans. And we were taken to barracks, in Dresden barrack, which was -- again, it's a terrible, horrible situation, because of the manifestation of the typhoid bacteria. There were-- people were like being killed and murdered by this bacteria by the hundreds. And they were lying there and they were not collected. The corpses were not collected. And there was absolutely no sanitation facilities, nothing at all. And that's how we were there. And lots of times, Germans said they wanted volunteers. My mom would never let me go out from her sight and volunteer. And then we suddenly heard that the Russians are coming in from both directions, from Dresden and from Prague. And we were liberated one day after the war ended, on May 9th, 1945, by Russian troops. And of course, some people-- for some people, it wasn't the end. Some people died even afterwards because of the food which was distributed by the Russian army, the heavy bread. And their weakened stomach just couldn't digest it, couldn't take it. And I guess my mother and I were fairly fortunate that we were among those who survived it. And as a matter of fact, we had some friends going to Theresienstadt and they saw the names of the people who were in the barracks and our names was listed there as being survived, because the ones who did not survive I think there was an asterisk next to their names. And we had to wait until clearance, until everything was cleared. And we had to be sanitized, of course. And we had to wait for a transport to take us back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Theresienstadt all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt, well, we were liberated on May 9th. And I think towards the end of the July it took about at least two and a half, three months to get us back to the transport and transport taking us back to Budapest. And in Budapest, there was a national committee for the caring of the deportees, the Jewish committee. And they had a reception for us. They took us into their offices. And this was a time when I heard about my father and my mother heard about my father as well, his tragic situation, what happened to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived in Budapest, what was the reaction of the population, non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from Budapest we were sent by this committee to Szolnok, where we used to live. And it was very mixed. Lots of people said that more came back than they left before in 1944, which was terrible to hear. And on the railroad trains you heard these expressions and the hate within their eyes and their voices. But there were some other ones who knew my father. And they came over in the streets of Szolnok to me and they expressed their sorrow and their condolences about the loss of my father. And they were people who obviously were either clients of his or knew him. Of course, they knew the whole family, as I said, because my family used to live there. And his great-grandfather, everybody lived in Szolnok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your home in Szolnok?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home was no longer there. Our furniture was no longer there. And some of the things, which were like my accordion, which I had just kept gotten for my birthday in 1944 and I gave it to my mother's aunt, that was taken. So we were all pretty much financially as well devastated at that time. And had it not been for the help of the American Joint Distribution Committee, I don't know what would have happened with us. I think we received clothing. We received food, received a lot of food from the American Jewish Committee, from the Joint. And my mother actually got a job, which was her first job ever in her life, working for the American Joint Committee in Szolnok, where she was like involved in the storage of the items and distribution of the items. And this is how she met my stepfather. See, my mother remarried in 1947, two years after the war. And the gentlemen she married to, he was a hazan. He was a cantor in another city. But his brother lived in Szolnok and he survived. His brother survived as well. So he came to Szolnok as a hazan. He was hired as a hazan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Bela, was his first name, Loebli, L-O-E-B-L-I. And he lost his wife in Auschwitz and his daughter. His daughter's name was Judith. And now you can connect how and why is my daughter named Judith as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he lost his family. He wanted to remarry. And in 1947, they remarried.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, my stepfather got a position, an offering from the synagogue in Gyor, in the city Gyor, G-Y-O-R, which was on the western side of Hungary, right at the Danube, at the River Danube. And this is where he accepted the position. And we eventually moved there in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back to your town Szolnok in 1945, your mother got a job. Did you go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, as a matter of fact, I had to hurry up, because I didn't want to lose two years, so I was prepared for the fourth year of my gymnasium. But I didn't take the test, so I was able to produce the first three years. But to pass the fourth year, I didn't go to this other private school. But rather I enrolled to the state school, to the state gymnasium in Szolnok. And I passed the fourth year there. And then in September, I joined the fifth year. And still I had one year. You know, I missed one year and that was that. And in 1947, of course, I had to get all my papers together and I transferred to a gymnasium, a state gymnasium in Gyor, where my stepfather was the cantor. And I graduated, again with honors, in 1949 after eight years of schooling from the gymnasium. And at that time, I decided I wanted to go to university. And after one year hiatus, I enrolled in 1950 to the Karl Marx University of Economic Sciences, where I wanted to be a certified economist. And that's what I did. And I graduated in 1954 from that university, again, with honors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you dealt with your father's death when you realized your mother's dream was true.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult. My strength actually came from my first cousin. And this first cousin was, as I mentioned to you, a lawyer. His name was Dr. Farkas, F-A-R-K-A-S, Shandor, Sandor. And he himself survived the forced labor battalions. And he came back and he showed up in 1945. And he took me aside and started talking to me and said, you know, this is how life is. This is what happens. It's a terrible thing what happened, but life must go on. And he tried to live his life. As well, he had a girlfriend and he lost his girlfriend as well. And from that point on, my cousin, and I was very proud of him, became the prosecutor in Szolnok of the war crimes. And as a matter of fact, he became quite controversial among Jewish circles, because he prosecuted a fairly prominent Jewish doctor, medical doctor, who was supposedly in Vienna, but he didn't take care of the inmates, of the Jewish people, but he just had his own family put on a higher pedestal. And I don't know what other things he was accused of, but this was one of the cases he tried, my first cousin tried. And he became very controversial about it, because lots of people felt that perhaps it wasn't the good thing for one Jew to bring to trial another Jew for war crimes. But he felt strongly about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any of your childhood friends after the war from Szolnok?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Lots of kids who went together with me in the same class or above me or lower in the class, lower than me, they perished in Auschwitz. Lots of them. A very talented friend of mine perished who was an orphan himself. He lost his father in I think World War I or some other time. And lots of them perished. But some of them came back. And some of us who came back were ardently Zionistic. We belonged and we joined the various Zionist organizations called Mapam, I believe, or something, mainly on the left side, like social democratic Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was 15, 16. And there was a very good friend of mine who actually without telling his parents, he escaped to Israel. And he became a very well known eye doctor, an ophthalmologist, who treated the president of Israel for his eye problems. And he still lives in Brooklyn, New York now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Gombos, G-O-M-B-O-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know when you were going to these gymnasiums, they were public schools now. They weren't private schools. So you were interacting with a lot of non-Jewish teenagers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever discussions about the war and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that much, no. Really there wasn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We pretty much kept for ourselves, though, even after the war. We were involved in the Zionist movement. I was involved in the Temple Youth movement, in some political organizations. But we were not talking too much about it, about the war or the extermination camps or the era of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never heard anti-Semitic remarks after?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I did not. I did not. Not really until the time of 1956, when the Hungarian revolution began. Then all those elements came to the surface, the ones who kept quiet under the Communist regime. See, in 1948, the Communist regime took over in Hungary from the coalition government. And the Communists, even though they were atheist, you know, they didn't believe in the religion, but they didn't tolerate anything against Jews or anything against minorities. So I didn't hear too much about it, except when I was up in the university. Then they found out about my Zionistic background. And they didn't like it. And that's why when I finished my university with high honors, they did not consider me as a good so-called candidate for a top position, because they don't trust my political instincts or--

speaker: interviewer
text: They being the heads of the university or the heads of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Those being the heads of the party.

speaker: interviewer
text: The government, the party.

speaker: interviewee
text: The heads of the party. See, like all these universities were pretty much controlled by--

speaker: interviewer
text: By the state.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the functionaries of the Communist party. So if you were not a Communist party member, no matter how good a student you were or how well you knew your stuff, it didn't help. You were delegated to lower type positions in Hungary. They discriminated against you, not because you were Jewish, but just simply because you were not a member of the elite, which was the party, the people affiliated with the Communist party.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened in your life after you graduated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I graduated in 1949, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I'm going way back in 1949. But after I graduated in 1954, we had several years we can really celebrate. 1953 I got married with my lovely wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Mary, or Maria. And we are married 42 years. And in 1954, I graduated. In 1955, my daughter was born. And then we moved to Gyor, where my mother lived with her husband. And in 1956, we came over to the United States. The revolution started. We came out to the United States. And even though I had a handicap because I did not know the language-- I didn't speak English. I learned French and Russian in high school and in gymnasium and also on the university. Russian, of course, we had to take. And French was my elective language. And also I learned Hebrew in the Zionist movement. But, you know, that wasn't enough when I arrived to United States. The interesting thing was when I arrived to United States, everybody thought I was not Jewish because I didn't speak Yiddish. And the way to understand this is that in Hungary, only the orthodox Jews really kept up with the Yiddish. The so-called Neolog or conservative Jews, they spoke the German like my mother, the high German language. And they could be understood. But here, they were all looking for people with Yiddish knowledge. And of course, I didn't have that. And I even like to forget the German as much as I could. But I was forced-- in my first job, I was forced to speak German because I applied for a job in a life insurance company, which is interesting story. The chief actuary hired me. And he hired me out of a Hungarian paper. A Hungarian paper advertised for intellectuals who had university degrees. And he applied to the advertisement. And I went in for the interview, but I didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what he did was there was a German lady in the accounting area. He brought her in to help translate his wordings and my answers. And then I got the job. I got the job in the accounting department as an assistant bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this lady helped me with all the tasks in the first few weeks. And I said-- Christine was her name. I don't remember her last name. And I said to myself and I told her, I said, look, I'm going to try to understand what they are telling me without you translating every word. And of course, I went to night schools here. There were special night schools which were by a Hungarian organization, I don't remember the exact name of it, for professionals like engineers and doctors and lawyers and economists and what have you, and at night. And I attended this for months and months to pick up the language. But basically, the way I picked up English language was from the television, interestingly. The way to speak and the mannerism and all this I picked up from the television shows. That was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now it's 1995. And what business are you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm still in the insurance business. I started out in the life insurance end of it. I'm in the property slash casualty insurance business, which is automobiles and homeowners and fire policies and that sort of thing. I work in a home office of a medium-sized company. And I'm the chief financial officer and treasurer, which means my responsibilities are the handling of the financials, the accounting, the budgeting, the allocation of the investments. And basically I'm the liaison to-- I'm the assistant to the president of the company in all matters relating to investments and finance and accounting. And we have several subsidiary corporations as well. And I have been in the casualty insurance business since I came out. And I'm very happy. I enjoy my position. I enjoy my work. And I enjoy the people I'm working with, and this is the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 Years later, when you think back to your experiences, are you angry at what happened and people?

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 Years later, I'm actually saddened by what happened and what cruelty people inflicted upon other people. And this is all due to the tremendous Nazi propaganda machinery, what Goebbels started. And I think people have a bad side within themselves. They like to see other people suffer. And this gives me great pain. And basically what great pain is caused by me, because I feel the tremendous loss of the people, the six million Jews who among themselves had many talented, tremendously talented men, women, and children, who could have been the next Einsteins or the next Mozarts of our century and their life was snuffed out doing the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to change the tape.
